title: Google Launches Gemini 3.5 Flash at I/O 2026, Betting Big on Agentic AI
summary: Google unveiled Gemini 3.5 Flash at Google I/O 2026, its fastest and most capable agentic model yet, signaling a strategic shift from conversational AI to autonomous AI agents.
content: At Google I/O 2026, Google introduced Gemini 3.5 Flash — its strongest agentic and coding model to date, now available to billions globally via the Gemini app, AI Mode in Search, Google Antigravity, and the Gemini API. The model outperforms Gemini 3.1 Pro on key benchmarks including Terminal-Bench 2.1 (76.2%) and MCP Atlas (83.6%), and runs at 4x the output speed of competing frontier models. Enterprise partners are already seeing impact: Shopify uses it to run parallel subagents for merchant forecasting, Macquarie Bank is piloting it for complex document reasoning, and Salesforce is integrating it into Agentforce for multi-step enterprise automation. Google also unveiled Gemini Spark — a personal AI agent powered by 3.5 Flash that runs 24/7 — with a beta rollout for Google AI Ultra subscribers beginning the following week. The 3.5 Pro model, designed to serve as an orchestrator for Flash-based subagents, is being tested internally and expected to launch publicly next month.

title: Anthropic's Claude Mythos Finds Bugs Faster Than Developers Can Patch Them
summary: Anthropic's Project Glasswing has used Claude Mythos Preview to uncover more than 10,000 critical vulnerabilities in one month — faster than the software industry can respond.
content: One month after launching Project Glasswing, Anthropic and roughly 50 partners have used Claude Mythos Preview to find more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities across critical internet infrastructure, including a flaw in wolfSSL — a cryptographic library used by billions of devices — that would have allowed attackers to forge bank and email certificates. The UK's AI Security Institute reports Mythos Preview is the first model to solve both of its cyber-attack simulation ranges end to end, while Mozilla found over 271 vulnerabilities in Firefox 150, more than ten times what Claude Opus 4.6 found in the previous version. The discovery rate has created an unprecedented bottleneck: Palo Alto Networks shipped five times its normal patch volume in its latest release, and several open-source maintainers have asked Anthropic to slow down disclosures because they cannot keep up. Of 1,752 high- or critical-severity findings independently reviewed so far, 90.6% were confirmed true positives. Anthropic warns that Mythos-class capabilities will soon be widely available across the industry, and has released Claude Security in public beta — which has already patched over 2,100 enterprise vulnerabilities — while withholding the Mythos model itself pending stronger safeguards.

title: Trump Scraps AI Safety Executive Order After Pushback from Musk and Zuckerberg
summary: President Trump abruptly canceled a White House ceremony to sign an AI safety executive order after Elon Musk and Mark Zuckerberg reportedly pressured him to back down.
content: President Trump canceled a scheduled signing ceremony for an AI executive order just hours before it was set to take place, after some top tech CEOs declined to attend with less than 24 hours notice — and after xAI's Elon Musk and Meta's Mark Zuckerberg reportedly urged him to scrap it. The draft order, obtained by POLITICO, would have created a voluntary system allowing federal agencies to evaluate frontier AI models up to 90 days before public release, with the goal of identifying cybersecurity vulnerabilities in banks, utilities, and critical infrastructure. The tech industry lobbied heavily against it, fearing safety reviews would delay model launches; AI labs had pushed for a 14-day review window versus the government's proposed 90 days. Trump told reporters he pulled the plug because 'I didn't like certain aspects of it,' adding he feared safety requirements could set the U.S. back in the AI race with China. The order's collapse comes amid internal White House tensions after Anthropic raised cybersecurity concerns about its Mythos model, and at the same time that Trump and Xi Jinping agreed at a summit to launch a formal intergovernmental AI dialogue.

title: California Governor Newsom Signs First-in-Nation AI Workforce Protection Order
summary: Governor Gavin Newsom issued a first-of-its-kind executive order directing California to prepare workers, small businesses, and communities for economic disruption caused by AI.
content: Governor Gavin Newsom signed a landmark executive order on May 21, 2026 making California the first state in the nation to formally address AI's expected disruption of the workforce. The order mobilizes state agencies, labor experts, economists, and universities to develop policies and data tools that can detect early warning signals of AI-driven job displacement across industries. Key directives include exploring severance and employment insurance reforms, worker ownership models such as employee-owned company structures, expanded workforce retraining programs, and a new dashboard tracking AI's sector-by-sector employment impact. The order also calls for revisions to California's WARN Act — which requires advance notice of mass layoffs — to better capture AI-driven workforce changes, and for a unified online platform to connect displaced workers with available services. Newsom framed the order as part of California's broader AI leadership strategy, noting that 33 of the top 50 private AI companies globally are based in the state, and that the next phase must ensure workers — not just tech companies — share in AI-driven productivity gains.

title: Cohere Releases Command A+ as First Fully Apache 2.0-Licensed Frontier AI Model
summary: Cohere launched Command A+, a 218-billion-parameter open-source model under a true Apache 2.0 license — a first for the company — enabling enterprises to run frontier-grade AI in fully private, self-hosted environments.
content: Canadian AI lab Cohere released Command A+, a 218-billion-parameter Sparse Mixture-of-Experts model optimized for complex reasoning, multimodal document processing, and agentic workflows, with only 25 billion parameters active at inference time. The model is the first Cohere release under a full Apache 2.0 open-source license, meaning any company or developer can use, modify, and commercialize it without licensing fees — a significant shift from the company's previous CC-BY-NC restrictions. Command A+ can run on a single NVIDIA Blackwell B200 GPU or two H100s in its W4A4 quantized format, achieving 375 tokens per second with just 113 milliseconds latency — up to 63% faster than the previous Command A. Benchmark performance jumped dramatically: on the τ²-Bench Telecom test, it rose from 37% to 85%, and on AIME 25 math, from 57% to 90%. It also features native citation generation — grounding every factual output to a source document — which Cohere says is essential for regulated industries like finance, healthcare, and legal. The release immediately gained traction through day-one integration with Hugging Face and vLLM.

title: Stanford 2026 AI Index: Capabilities Accelerating, US-China Gap Effectively Closed
summary: Stanford HAI's annual AI Index report finds AI is not plateauing but accelerating, organizational adoption has hit 88%, and the performance gap between US and Chinese models has nearly vanished.
content: The Stanford HAI 2026 AI Index Report documents a year of rapid AI acceleration: on the SWE-bench coding benchmark, model performance rose from 60% to near 100% in a single year, and organizational AI adoption reached 88%. The U.S.-China AI model performance gap has effectively closed — as of March 2026, Anthropic's top model leads the best Chinese model by just 2.7%, with the two trading the top position multiple times since early 2025. U.S. private AI investment hit $285.9 billion in 2025 — 23 times China's — but the number of AI researchers moving to the U.S. has collapsed by 89% since 2017, including an 80% drop in the past year alone. Generative AI reached 53% population adoption within three years, faster than the PC or the internet, with the estimated annual consumer value of AI tools reaching $172 billion in early 2026 — tripling in value per user versus 2025. On the darker side, documented AI incidents rose to 362 (up from 233 in 2024), responsible AI benchmarking remains spotty relative to capability benchmarks, and only 31% of Americans trust their own government to regulate AI effectively — the lowest of any country surveyed.
